title: New (Inter)Faces: Polymers and Inorganic Materials
abstract:
Research News
New (Inter)Faces: Polymers and Inorganic
Materials**
By Mark J. MacLachlan, Ian Manners,* and Geoffrey A. Ozin*
1. New (Inter)Faces
A revolution in materials research is occurring at the in-
terface of polymer and inorganic materials chemistry.[1]
Two major sub-disciplines of chemistry, that not so long
ago seemed self-contained, disconnected, and mature, are
now being integrated in novel, imaginative, and important
ways. Early explorations at the boundary between the
fields are unveiling new classes of polymer±inorganic hy-
brid materials with structures and compositions unparal-
leled in materials science. The coalition of synthetic con-
cepts and methods provides an approach to nanocomposite
materials in which the interface between polymer and inor-
ganic constituents is under molecular control. This alliance
enables the design of hierarchical structures that may ex-
hibit properties and functions unattainable with polymer
and inorganic components alone. In this Research News ar-
ticle, some recent materials research occurring at the
boundary of polymer/oligomer and inorganic materials
chemistry is examined. This is intended to highlight the
scientific vitality, excitement and opportunities that abound
in this emerging field.
There are many aspects of the work that are especially
appealing and bode well for a vibrant future of research in
this interdisciplinary area. The most attractive feature of
the work is that polymer inorganic hybrid materials can ex-
ploit the chemical and physical attributes of both compo-
nents in a new way. Thus, when integrated into a designer
architecture with command of the interface between con-
stituents at the molecular scale, the composite can display
unique behavior not available to the polymer and inorganic
parts alone. Another aspect that is particularly unusual
concerns the amalgamation of structures and length scales
typically associated with polymers and inorganics to create
functional hybrid materials with hierarchical architectures
that are unprecedented in materials science. Furthermore,
the intentional combination of polymeric and inorganic
building units in a self-assembly synthesis provides control
over interfaces at the molecular scale. This new approach
to synthesizing polymer±inorganic composite materials
from the ªbottom-upº rather than fabricating them in the
traditional way from the ªtop-downº, offers new ways to
introduce function and form into materials for possible use
in dental and bone implants, corrosion protection and bat-
tery electrolytes, dielectrics and food packaging, and vehi-
cles for controlled chemical release.[2]
2. Concepts and Examples
Some examples that illustrate the benefits of integrating
polymer/oligomer and solid-state materials chemistry are
briefly mentioned in the following paragraphs. To begin,
the impressive morphologies of microphase separated di-
block and triblock copolymers have been utilized as tem-
plates to direct the hydrolytic polycondensation of metal
alkoxides into mesoporous metal oxide, composite materi-
als.[3,4] These hybrid materials have structures that can be
considered as ªcastsº of the templating mesophase. The
polymer template can be removed from the composite to
produce a mesoporous inorganic material that can function
as a ªmoldº for the polymerization of an organic mono-
mer.[5] Removal of the inorganic by a chemical etching pro-
cedure recreates the mesoporous material but now in the
form of a membrane made from the newly formed poly-
mer.
An extension of this synthetic paradigm, but at a higher
length scale, makes use of a colloidal crystal, comprised of
monodisperse polystyrene spheres, as a template for creat-
ing a macroporous inorganic crystal.[6,7] The process begins
with the infiltration of an inorganic precursor, like a metal
alkoxide, into the octahedral and tetrahedral interstitial
void spaces of a latex colloidal crystal. The metal alkoxide
undergoes hydrolytic polycondensation to form a compos-
ite metal oxide±polymer colloidal crystal. Solvent extrac-
Adv. Mater. 2000, 12, No. 9
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 2000
0935-9648/00/0905-0675 $ 17.50+.50/0

[*] Prof. G. A. Ozin, Prof. I. Manners, Dr. M. J. MacLachlan
Inorganic Polymers and Inorganic Materials Research Groups
Department of Chemistry, University of Toronto
80 St. George Street, Toronto, ON M5S 3H6 (Canada)
[**] GAO and IM gratefully acknowledge the Natural Sciences and Engi-
neering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) for financial support
of their work. MJM thanks NSERC for a postgraduate scholarship
(1995±99). IM expresses his gratitude to the Ontario Government for
a PREA Award (1999±2004), NSERC for an E. W. R. Steacie Fellow-
ship (1997±99), and the University of Toronto for a McLean Fellow-
ship (1997±2003). GAO acknowledges the Canada Council for the
award of an Issac Walton Killam Foundation Fellowship (1995±97),
and he also thanks Mobil Technology Company, UOP, and the Dow
Chemical Company for their monetary support of his research over
the years.
 676
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 2000
0935-9648/00/0905-0676 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 2000, 12, No. 9
tion facilitates the removal of the polymer spheres to leave
behind an inorganic shell with the structure of an inverted
colloidal crystal or inverted opal (Fig. 1). The diameter of
the spheres comprising the template can span the range
20±1500 nm and the composition of periodic macroporous
inorganic crystals made in this way include silica, alumina,
titania, and zirconia. This strategy has been extended to
create mixed metal oxide, non-oxide, and metallic inverted
colloidal crystals. For example, the reaction of oxalic acid
with a nickel acetate impregnated polystyrene colloidal
crystal template precipitates an insoluble nickel oxalate
replica scaffold, which on calcination converts to nickel
oxide and on hydrogen reduction to a periodic macropor-
ous crystal having nanocrystalline nickel walls.[8] The mate-
rial has been found to function as an electrochemical ca-
pacitor that stores charge in an amount proportional to the
accessible nickel surface area (25±110 m2 g±1). Metallic in-
verted colloidal crystals may find utility as porous elec-
trodes, electrocatalysts, sensors, and photonic crystals.
A similar colloidal crystal templating strategy has been
used to create ordered macroporous polymers.[9] In this
synthesis, the template is comprised of monodisperse silica
spheres and the polymer (divinylbenzene±ethyleneglycol
dimethacrylate copolymer) is imbibed within the intersti-
tial void spaces. Etching of the silica spheres from the poly-
mer±silica composite leaves behind a crosslinked, yet flex-
ible
and
shrinkable,
polymer
shell
that
essentially
replicates that of the silica colloidal crystal (Fig. 2).
Through careful selection of organic and inorganic func-
tionality located on the internal surface of periodic macro-
porous polymers it should prove possible to develop a new
generation of stationary phases with utility for chromato-
graphic separations, chiral synthesis, and enzyme immobili-
zation. A creative amalgamation of block co-polymer and
polymer colloidal crystal templating has led to macropor-
ous crystals built of mesoporous silica.[10] These have been
patterned on the micrometer scale using the technique of

crystals. A) A crystal synthesized by templating polystyrene spheres with ra-
dii of 987 ± 20 nm. B) An expansion of the structure shown in A) reveals
small holes connecting the air spheres (left arrow) and small voids at the tet-
rahedral holes formed where four air spheres intersect (right arrow). C) A
crystal synthesized with spheres having radii of 172 ± 8 nm. Reprinted from
[7] with permission. Copyright 1998, American Association for the Ad-
vancement of Science.
Fig. 2. SEM images of macroporous polymers. Reprinted from [9] with per-
mission. Copyright 1999, American Association for the Advancement of
Science.

By Mark J. MacLachlan, Ian Manners,* and Geoffrey A. Ozin*
A revolution in materials research is occurring at the in-
terface of polymer and inorganic materials chemistry.[1]
Two major sub-disciplines of chemistry, that not so long
ago seemed self-contained, disconnected, and mature, are
now being integrated in novel, imaginative, and important
ways. Early explorations at the boundary between the
fields are unveiling new classes of polymer±inorganic hy-
brid materials with structures and compositions unparal-
leled in materials science. The coalition of synthetic con-
cepts and methods provides an approach to nanocomposite
materials in which the interface between polymer and inor-
ganic constituents is under molecular control. This alliance
enables the design of hierarchical structures that may ex-
hibit properties and functions unattainable with polymer
and inorganic components alone. In this Research News ar-
ticle, some recent materials research occurring at the
boundary of polymer/oligomer and inorganic materials
chemistry is examined. This is intended to highlight the
scientific vitality, excitement and opportunities that abound

appealing and bode well for a vibrant future of research in
this interdisciplinary area. The most attractive feature of
the work is that polymer inorganic hybrid materials can ex-
ploit the chemical and physical attributes of both compo-
nents in a new way. Thus, when integrated into a designer
architecture with command of the interface between con-
stituents at the molecular scale, the composite can display
unique behavior not available to the polymer and inorganic
parts alone. Another aspect that is particularly unusual
concerns the amalgamation of structures and length scales
typically associated with polymers and inorganics to create
functional hybrid materials with hierarchical architectures
that are unprecedented in materials science. Furthermore,
the intentional combination of polymeric and inorganic
building units in a self-assembly synthesis provides control
over interfaces at the molecular scale. This new approach
to synthesizing polymer±inorganic composite materials
from the ªbottom-upº rather than fabricating them in the
traditional way from the ªtop-downº, offers new ways to
introduce function and form into materials for possible use
in dental and bone implants, corrosion protection and bat-
tery electrolytes, dielectrics and food packaging, and vehi-
cles for controlled chemical release.[2]
Some examples that illustrate the benefits of integrating
polymer/oligomer and solid-state materials chemistry are
briefly mentioned in the following paragraphs. To begin,
the impressive morphologies of microphase separated di-
block and triblock copolymers have been utilized as tem-
plates to direct the hydrolytic polycondensation of metal
alkoxides into mesoporous metal oxide, composite materi-
als.[3,4] These hybrid materials have structures that can be
considered as ªcastsº of the templating mesophase. The
polymer template can be removed from the composite to
produce a mesoporous inorganic material that can function
as a ªmoldº for the polymerization of an organic mono-
mer.[5] Removal of the inorganic by a chemical etching pro-
cedure recreates the mesoporous material but now in the

tion facilitates the removal of the polymer spheres to leave
behind an inorganic shell with the structure of an inverted
colloidal crystal or inverted opal (Fig. 1). The diameter of
the spheres comprising the template can span the range
20±1500 nm and the composition of periodic macroporous
inorganic crystals made in this way include silica, alumina,
titania, and zirconia. This strategy has been extended to
create mixed metal oxide, non-oxide, and metallic inverted
colloidal crystals. For example, the reaction of oxalic acid
with a nickel acetate impregnated polystyrene colloidal
crystal template precipitates an insoluble nickel oxalate
replica scaffold, which on calcination converts to nickel
oxide and on hydrogen reduction to a periodic macropor-
ous crystal having nanocrystalline nickel walls.[8] The mate-
rial has been found to function as an electrochemical ca-
pacitor that stores charge in an amount proportional to the
accessible nickel surface area (25±110 m2 g±1). Metallic in-
verted colloidal crystals may find utility as porous elec-
trodes, electrocatalysts, sensors, and photonic crystals.
A similar colloidal crystal templating strategy has been
used to create ordered macroporous polymers.[9] In this
synthesis, the template is comprised of monodisperse silica
spheres and the polymer (divinylbenzene±ethyleneglycol
dimethacrylate copolymer) is imbibed within the intersti-
tial void spaces. Etching of the silica spheres from the poly-
mer±silica composite leaves behind a crosslinked, yet flex-
replicates that of the silica colloidal crystal (Fig. 2).
Through careful selection of organic and inorganic func-
tionality located on the internal surface of periodic macro-
porous polymers it should prove possible to develop a new
generation of stationary phases with utility for chromato-
graphic separations, chiral synthesis, and enzyme immobili-
zation. A creative amalgamation of block co-polymer and
polymer colloidal crystal templating has led to macropor-
ous crystals built of mesoporous silica.[10] These have been
patterned on the micrometer scale using the technique of
Fig. 1. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of TiO2 inverted opal
crystals. A) A crystal synthesized by templating polystyrene spheres with ra-
dii of 987 ± 20 nm. B) An expansion of the structure shown in A) reveals
small holes connecting the air spheres (left arrow) and small voids at the tet-
rahedral holes formed where four air spheres intersect (right arrow). C) A
crystal synthesized with spheres having radii of 172 ± 8 nm. Reprinted from
[7] with permission. Copyright 1998, American Association for the Ad-
Fig. 2. SEM images of macroporous polymers. Reprinted from [9] with per-
mission. Copyright 1999, American Association for the Advancement of
micromolding in capillaries.[11] In an
analogous way, a dual templating
strategy has produced the first exam-
ples of macroporous crystals with
zeolitic walls.[12] These materials offer
the benefit of rapid mass transport of
reactants to and from active zeolitic
crystal sites that comprise the walls
Block copolymers have also been
used as hosts for inorganic materi-
als.[13] A film of poly(tert-butylac-
rylate)-block-poly(2-cinnamoylethyl
methacrylate) (PtBA-b-PCEMA) as-
sembled to give hexagonally packed
cylinders of PtBA inside the PCEMA
loosely packed poly(acrylic acid) in-
side the channels. These nanochan-
nels could then be filled with solid-
state materials, such as CdS and
Fe2O3, yielding well-defined nano-
particles (Fig. 3). This approach may
generate magnetic and semiconduct-
ing polymer films with monodisperse
polymer±inorganic composites pro-
vides a synthetic pathway to hierarch-
ical materials on length scales that
are entirely new and with structures that are highly unusu-
al. Perceived utility of these materials are wide ranging and
include photonic crystals, chemical sensors, fuel cell elec-
trodes, battery electrolytes, solar cell photocathodes, cata-
lysts, membranes, and chromatography supports.
Oligo(ethyleneoxide)-based lyotropic liquid crystals and
lithium salts have been imbibed within the channels of me-
soporous silica to create a new class of ionically conducting
hybrid materials that can function as an electrolyte in a
lithium solid-state battery.[14] Poly(phenolformaldehyde)
has been grown within the channels of mesoporous silica
materials from which the silica has been etched away to
create poly(phenolformaldehyde) fibers with a width con-
trolled by the diameter of the channels and length deter-
mined by the dimensions of the host particles.[15] This pro-
cedure has led to single strands or bundles of polymer
mesofibers with aspect ratios greater than 104 (Fig. 4). A
similar process has been used to synthesize composites of
mesoporous silica with poly(acrylonitrile) and poly(ani-
line).[16] The latter proved to be intrinsically electrically
conducting, while pyrolysis of the former led to electrically
conducting carbon-based composites.
Ionically and electrically conducting and insulating poly-
mers have been intercalated into a wide variety of inorgan-
ic layered host materials.[17] Typical examples of the layered
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 2000
0935-9648/00/0905-0677 $ 17.50+.50/0
Fig. 3. A) Scheme illustrating the preparation of nanochannels in the PtBA-b-PCEMA polymer membrane.
B) TEM image of poly(acrylic acid) channels containing CdS nanoparticles. Reprinted from [13] with per-
mission. Copyright 1999, American Chemical Society.
Fig. 4. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of poly(phenolfor-
maldehyde) mesofibers extracted from the channels of MCM-41. The fibers
were imaged on a carbon grid using a negative staining technique with ura-
nyl acetate. Scale bar = 100 nm. Reprinted from [15] with permission. Copy-
right 1998, Royal Society of Chemistry.
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 2000
0935-9648/00/0905-0678 $ 17.50+.50/0
host include mica-type silicates, molybdenum trioxide, iron
oxychloride, and metal sulfides. Polymer±inorganic nano-
laminate materials of this type are of considerable interest
as battery cathode materials. Layered inorganic materials
can also be exfoliated in solution into single-sheet colloidal
particles.[18] Through chemical control of colloidal interac-
tions, these materials can be reassembled, in the presence
of a wide range of polymers, to create superlattices of poly-
mers and inorganics. Because of the molecular scale control
of the interface between organic and inorganic components
properties of these hybrid materials are expected to be
quite interesting. Layer-by-layer electrostatic assembly of
poly(electrolytes) of alternating charge has been utilized to
form ultrathin polymer superlattices on inorganic sur-
faces.[19] An extension of this methodology has been effec-
tively employed to make a diverse collection of polymer±
inorganic composite films and some have been integrated
into functional devices, such as Zerner diodes, metal±insu-
lator±nanocluster±insulator±metal (MINIM) single elec-
tron transistors, and ultrathin-film lithium batteries.[20]
Polymer films can be grown directly from inorganic sur-
faces that have been functionalized with a self-assembled
monolayer of polymerizable monomer.[21] In this strategy
an alkylalkoxysilane or alkanethiol with a terminal olefinic
moiety may be anchored to a silicon, silica or gold sub-
strate. Platinum-catalyzed hydrosilation of a hydrosiloxane
polymer facilitates the chemical attachment of the polymer
to the substrate. Self-assembled films of this type that con-
tain cyclodextrin, resorcinol, or calixarene receptor sites
have been grown off the surface of the gold electrode of a
quartz crystal microbalance. Composite polymer±gold±
quartz crystal electrodes have been demonstrated to func-
tion as a mass sensitive chemoselective sensor. Monodis-
perse nanoparticles may be coated with initiators for atom
transfer radical polymerization (ATRP), and these macro-
initiators may be used to graft polymers onto the inorganic
spheres.[22] Thus, ªhairyº poly(styrene)±SiO2 nanoparticles
may self-assemble into densely packed arrays of inorganic
nanoparticles with controlled packing dimensions (Fig. 5).
The potential to extend this methodology to shaped silica
particles, particularly hexagonal mesoporous silica gyroids,
rods and hollow helicoids, is intriguing as a route to new
types of nanocomposite membranes.
Nanocrystalline inorganics, such as titania and alumina,
have been shown to function as plasticizers for poly(ethyl-
eneoxide)±lithium salt electrolytes.[23] The nanocrystalline
inorganic phase in the composite materials is able to stabi-
lize the glassy state of the polymer at ambient temperatures
and facilitates room temperature lithium ion conductivity.
Selective interactions between nanoparticle metal surface
sites and oxygen atoms of the polymer and anions of the
lithium salt are considered responsible for the improved
performance of the composite as a battery electrolyte.
These inorganic±polymer hybrids offer mechanical, corro-
sion resistance and safety advantages over polymers that
have been plasticized with organic solvents, which makes
them very attractive as solid electrolytes for lithium batter-
ies. Polymers have also been used as capping agents in the
synthesis of metal nanoclusters.[24] Their presence in a syn-
thesis serves to control the nucleation and growth of the
clusters and the stabilization of a single cluster size. Thiol
terminated poly(ethyleneoxide) has been used to cap and
stabilize gold nanoclusters.[25] Lithium salts have been in-
corporated into these polymer±gold cluster hybrid materi-
als and shown to function as a new kind of solid electrolyte
for lithium batteries. Microphase-separated block copoly-
mers, synthesized by ring opening metathesis polymeriza-
tion, with one block containing a metal precursor, have
been used for the creation of periodic arrays of metal nano-
structures stabilized in a polymer matrix.[26]
One of the most widely studied classes of intercalation
compounds for advanced battery applications is the layered
transition-metal disulfides. Intercalation is often accompa-
nied by electron injection into the conduction band of the
host and concomitant insertion of cations between the
sheets. The difficulty of achieving high lithium loading, the
mechanical destruction of the cathode through repeated
discharging±charging intercalation cycles, as well as slow
lithium diffusion within the material all create major stum-
bling blocks for the use of metal sulfide materials as cath-
odes in lithium batteries. An interesting approach for over-
coming these difficulties is to design metal sulfide/polymer
nanocomposites, which combine the properties of inorganic
metal sulfides with the processability and high lithium dif-
fusivity of a polymer. For instance, poly(ethyleneoxide)
Fig. 5. A) Scheme illustrating the use of silica nanospheres as macroinitia-
tors for ATRP. B) Close-packed ªhairyº silica nanospheres obtained after
polymerization. Scale bar = 100 nm. Reprinted from [22] with permission.
Copyright 1999, American Chemical Society.
(PEO) composites of lamellar molybdenum sulfides have
been synthesized by mixing aqueous solutions of exfoliated
metal sulfide sheets and PEO.[27] The interlayer distance
between the MoS2 sheets was observed to increase as a
function of the amount of PEO incorporated into the mate-
rial. Improvements in both the lithium ion diffusivity and
the electrical conductivity were obtained. In a similar vein,
exfoliated single sheet colloids of NbSe2 have been reas-
sembled with various polymers, such as poly(vinylpyrroli-
done), poly(ethyleneoxide), and poly(ethyleneglycol) to
sheets were found to retain their superconducting proper-
ties after intercalation of the polymer. The name ªplastic
superconductorsº was given to this new class of materials.
Similarly, exfoliated single-sheet colloids of SnS2 and MoS2
have been reassembled with poly(allylamine hydrochlo-
ride) in a layer-by-layer fashion to make electrostatic
superlattices comprised of alternating layers of metal disul-
fide and polymer.[29] Films formed in this way were found
to be quite smooth, as established by
atomic force microscopy (AFM).
Polymers with inorganic elements in
the backbone represent an area of grow-
branch of polymer science offers inter-
esting possibilities for innovation and
invention at the polymer-inorganic mate-
rials interface. For example, poly(ferro-
cenylsilane) materials have recently been
synthesized by ring-opening polymeriza-
tion (ROP) of strained [1]ferroceno-
polymers have been found to exhibit a
range of interesting properties and syn-
thetic methods have been developed
which allow the production of diblock
and triblock polymers in which the poly
(ferrocenylsilane) comprises one of the
blocks. These composite block copoly-
mers have been found to undergo micro-
phase separation, and, in an appropriate
solvent, to form interesting morphologies
based on isolated or collections of spheri-
cal and cylindrical micelles.[31] Recently,
the first water-soluble neutral and ionic
poly(ferrocenylsilanes) have been pre-
pared and the cationic poly(ferrocene)
polyelectrolytes offer opportunities for
the layer-by-layer self-assembly of elec-
trostatic superlattices, which could be
used for the fabrication of magnetic
multilayers for data storage, patterned,
redox-active thin-film electronic devices
and designer ceramic architectures.[30,32]
Metal-containing polymers may also
function as precursors to new genera-
tions of ceramic materials. Crosslinked poly(ferrocenylsi-
lane) materials synthesized by ROP of spirocyclic [1]ferro-
cenophanes have been found to be excellent precursors to
shaped magnetic ceramics formed in greater than 90 % ce-
ramic yield.[33] These nanocomposite materials have a
structure based upon a dispersion of iron nanoclusters in a
graphite/amorphous silicon carbide matrix (Fig. 6), and the
particles can be size-tailored to be either superparamag-
netic or ferromagnetic. ROP of [1]ferrocenophanes has
also been conducted within the channels of hexagonal me-
soporous silica and the encapsulated poly(ferrocene) meso-
fibers transformed to magnetic nanostructures by con-
trolled thermal treatment.[34, 35]
The fusion of polymer and inorganic materials chemistry
can be considered to have originated in the natural world
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 2000
0935-9648/00/0905-0679 $ 17.50+.50/0
Fig. 6. A) Scheme showing the synthesis of the shaped ceramics from crosslinked poly(ferrocenylsi-
lanes). B) TEM image of Fe nanoclusters in a Si/C matrix obtained from pyrolysis of a film of cross-
linked poly(ferrocenylsilane) at 550 C. Reprinted from [33] with permission. Copyright 2000, Ameri-
can Association for the Advancement of Science.
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 2000
0935-9648/00/0905-0680 $ 17.50+.50/0
where biopolymers emerged over evolutionary time scales
with the ability to control nucleation, growth, and form of
inorganics as biominerals and structural biomaterials.[36]
The mechanical and physicochemical properties of these
hybrid materials is determined by the structure, composi-
tion and disposition of mineral and macromolecular com-
ponents, and the complementarity of charge, geometry, and
stereochemistry at the interface between these phases.[37]
This is ªnature's epitaxyº. Biological polymer±inorganic
hybrids have long been studied in the biomineralization
community. Ideas concerning the formation of biological
minerals now emerge in biomimetic inorganic materials
fields that both hold rich promise for future innovation.
Some literature examples that illustrate the biomimetic
approach to polymer±inorganic nanocomposites are briefly
described in the following paragraphs. For instance, com-
posite nanolaminates can be synthesized in a single-step
hydrolytic polycondensation process. The method involves
the co-assembly of a templating surfactant mesophase, a
mixture of a tetraalkoxysilane, a silicon alkoxide containing
a terminally bonded polymerizable group, an organic cross-
linking agent, and a radical initiator.[38] Evaporation-in-
duced self-assembly of this mixture creates a well-ordered
polymer±silica nanolaminate film. Materials formed by this
biomimetic pathway have an architecture that resembles
that of the nacre of abalone shell.
In a biomimetic fashion, block copolymers have been ob-
cleaved mica surfaces to give nanoscale patterns, which
have then been utilized as lithographic masks for making
periodic nanoarrays of inorganic materials, such as titania
and gold.[38] Polystyrene±polybutadiene block copolymers
that phase-separate into hexagonal or cylindrical microdo-
mains have been used as masks to etch patterns into silicon
nitride (Fig. 7).[40] Blends of hydrophobic and hydrophilic
polymers have been found to undergo phase separation
when exposed to a soft-lithographically defined pattern of
self-assembled alkanethiols on a gold surface.[41] Solvent
removal of one of the components of the polymer blend
leaves behind a pattern of the other component on the gold
substrate that can be used as a lithographic mask for creat-
Finally, a fascinating recent example that illustrates the
importance of complementarity of structure and geometry
at the interface between polymers and inorganics as well as
the pivotal role of crystalline mesoporosity is in the cata-
lyzed growth of oriented polymer nanofibers in hexagonal
mesoporous silica.[42] In this system, the advantages of a
well-defined and tunable metallocene catalyst anchored to
silica have been combined with the tailorable diameter,
uniformly organized and extended channels of fiber-shaped
mesoporous silica. In the presence of ethylene, this nano-
channel catalyst produces exceptionally high molecular
weight (6 200 000) crystalline nanofiber bundles of linear
polyethylene with a diameter of 30±50 nm. It is envisioned
that the crystalline mesoporosity of the silica framework
can function as a ªnanoextruderº for the synthesis of ori-
ented polymer nanofibers for a range of common polymers,
thereby endowing them with new bulk properties. By con-
trast, a metallocene catalyst attached to a silica aerogel
support leads to a structurally inhomogeneous polyethyl-
ene with a broad molecular weight distribution.
Not so long ago in materials chemistry it seemed that
there was only ªroom at the bottomº. The trend was to
synthesize and organize nanoscopic materials. As we enter
the new millennium it is becoming abundantly clear that
there is ªroom at the topº. This conceptual change is a con-
sequence of the realization that self-assembly, templating,
patterning, capping, wiring, layering, and molding methods
have expanded the materials chemist's tool box to include
synthesis and organization of materials at ªallº length
scales.[43,44] Hierarchy has been introduced into materials
chemistry and purely synthetic integrated chemical, physi-
cal, and biological systems, designed to achieve a particular
function, are becoming a reality.
The boundary between polymers/oligomers and inorgan-
ics and the relative disposition of components in the struc-
ture are expected to profoundly influence the properties of
composites. Because of this it is anticipated that an amalga-
mation of research methods in inorganic polymer/oligomer

and solid-state materials chemistry will likely enable the
development of novel self-assembling hybrid architectures.

ing and ultimately tailored for the technological needs of
